layersIntro: With many AI-powered tools designed for consumers, a determined user could reproduce about 70% of the experience with some good prompts. This is not true with Alignd. In our technical architecture, five structural layers sit between the user and the raw model, each adding value the model cannot supply on its own:
cols: Why a frontier model alone can't do it

layers.name: 1. Corpus council (multi-persona deliberation)
layers.adds: Independent expert perspectives + clinical escalation guardrails synthesized into one answer
layers.why: A single model produces one voice with no internal check; “prompt-stuffing” to “act as a panel” is unreliable and unauditable

layers.name: 2. Proprietary knowledge corpus + retrieval
layers.adds: Traceable content and answers grounded in a body of 30+ years of clinical interactions and research.
layers.why: The model only knows its public training data; it has no access to Alignd's clinical knowledge.

layers.name: 3. Domain modeling (COM-B program structure)
layers.adds: Plans are structured and mapped to a validated, clinically-coherent 8-week plan schema
layers.why: A model returns prose; it has no concept of a program lifecycle, week themes, or task variants to enforce

layers.name: 4. Longitudinal memory & state
layers.adds: An evolving per-family record: intake, plan versions, weekly check-ins, conversation history
layers.why: Frontier chats are stateless between sessions and forget everything once context scrolls off

layers.name: 5. Lifecycle engagement engine
layers.adds: 28+ behaviorally-triggered nudges across email, SMS, and in-app that drive adherence
layers.why: The model can't reach out, can't track time, and can't run a multi-week accountability loop
